article.title: Attackers Hijack Axios npm Account and Push Cross-Platform RAT to Millions of Developers in Three-Hour Window
article.summary: A compromised maintainer account let attackers inject a remote access trojan into two Axios releases, potentially exposing any CI/CD pipeline or developer machine that ran npm install during a three-hour window.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Attackers hijacked the npm account of the lead maintainer of Axios, one of the most widely used HTTP client libraries in the JavaScript ecosystem, and published two malicious versions that silently installed a cross-platform remote access trojan on any system that ran `npm install` during an approximately three-hour window on March 31, 2026. With roughly 100 million weekly downloads, the incident represents one of the largest npm supply chain compromises on record, according to [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/hackers-compromise-axios-npm-package-to-drop-cross-platform-malware/).

## What Happened

At 00:21 UTC on March 31, the compromised account published `axios@1.14.1`, followed by `axios@0.30.4` at 01:00 UTC, covering both the current and legacy release branches. Neither version contained changes to the Axios source code itself. Instead, the attacker injected a single new dependency, `plain-crypto-js@4.2.1`, into the `package.json` file, as reported by [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/hackers-compromise-axios-npm-package-to-drop-cross-platform-malware/). That package had been pre-staged roughly 18 hours earlier with a clean initial version before the malicious payload was swapped in just minutes before the Axios releases.

The malicious dependency used a `postinstall` hook to execute a 4,209-byte dropper script that detected the host operating system and fetched a platform-specific RAT payload, according to [Snyk](https://snyk.io/blog/axios-npm-package-compromised-supply-chain-attack-delivers-cross-platform/). On macOS, the malware dropped a binary disguised as an Apple system daemon at `/Library/Caches/com.apple.act.mond`. On Windows, it copied PowerShell to `%PROGRAMDATA%\wt.exe` to evade endpoint detection tools. On Linux, it downloaded a Python script to `/tmp/ld.py` and launched it as an orphaned background process.

All three payloads beaconed to a command-and-control server at `sfrclak[.]com` on port 8000, with traffic disguised as npm registry requests. The RAT supported arbitrary command execution, shell access, filesystem enumeration, and binary injection, as detailed by [Snyk](https://snyk.io/blog/axios-npm-package-compromised-supply-chain-attack-delivers-cross-platform/).

After execution, the dropper erased its own traces: it deleted the setup script, removed the modified `package.json`, and replaced it with a clean stub file to hinder forensic analysis, according to [Socket](https://socket.dev/blog/axios-npm-package-compromised).

## How the Account Was Compromised

The attacker gained control of the npm account belonging to Jason Saayman (`jasonsaayman`), the primary Axios maintainer, and changed the associated email address to `ifstap@proton.me`, as reported by [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/hackers-compromise-axios-npm-package-to-drop-cross-platform-malware/). The attacker bypassed the normal GitHub Actions OIDC-based publishing workflow by publishing directly via the npm CLI using a long-lived access token. Gaps in the publishing workflow, including continued use of a long-lived npm token alongside trusted publishing, enabled the unauthorized access, according to [Socket](https://socket.dev/blog/axios-npm-package-compromised).

The compromised maintainer's elevated permissions initially prevented other team members from revoking the attacker's access, further complicating rapid remediation.

## Who Is Behind It

John Hultquist, chief analyst at Google Threat Intelligence Group, attributed the attack to UNC1069, a North Korean threat actor that targets cryptocurrency exchanges, financial software developers, and venture capital firms, according to [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/hackers-compromise-axios-npm-package-to-drop-cross-platform-malware/). Researchers also noted that the macOS RAT's internal name, "macWebT," references malware previously associated with BlueNoroff, a North Korean group specializing in financially motivated cyberattacks, though a definitive attribution has not been confirmed.

## Detection and Response

Socket's automated scanning system flagged the malicious package within approximately six minutes of publication, according to [Snyk](https://snyk.io/blog/axios-npm-package-compromised-supply-chain-attack-delivers-cross-platform/). Multiple security firms, including Snyk, Huntress, Endor Labs, StepSecurity, and Elastic, independently confirmed the compromise. npm removed both malicious Axios versions by 03:29 UTC, roughly three hours after the first was published.

Within Huntress's partner base alone, at least 135 endpoints across all three operating systems contacted the attacker's command-and-control infrastructure during the exposure window, as reported by [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/hackers-compromise-axios-npm-package-to-drop-cross-platform-malware/). The full blast radius remains unknown, as any CI/CD pipeline, developer workstation, or automated build system that resolved to the malicious versions during the window may have been silently compromised.

## What We Don't Know

- The exact number of systems that executed the malicious payload during the three-hour window has not been disclosed.
- Whether the attacker exfiltrated data or credentials from compromised systems before the RAT was detected remains unclear.
- How the attacker obtained the long-lived npm token used to publish the malicious versions has not been publicly explained.
- The full scope of secondary compromised packages is still being investigated; at least three additional packages (`@shadanai/openclaw` and `@qqbrowser/openclaw-qbot`) were found to contain the same malicious dependency, according to [Snyk](https://snyk.io/blog/axios-npm-package-compromised-supply-chain-attack-delivers-cross-platform/).

## Remediation

Security researchers recommend that any organization that ran `npm install` during the exposure window should treat affected systems as fully compromised. Immediate steps include downgrading to `axios@1.14.0` or `axios@0.30.3`, rotating all credentials and API keys on affected machines, scanning for the known file-based indicators of compromise, and reviewing network logs for connections to `sfrclak[.]com`, according to [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/hackers-compromise-axios-npm-package-to-drop-cross-platform-malware/). Organizations should also audit their dependency management practices, pin package versions, commit lockfiles, and consider using `npm ci` instead of `npm install` in CI/CD pipelines.

The incident is the second major npm supply chain attack in March 2026, following the [CanisterWorm compromise of the Trivy security scanner](/article/2026-03/23-trivy-supply-chain-compromise-spawns-canisterworm-the-first-npm-worm-to-use-blockchain-for-command-and-control) that used blockchain-based command and control. Together, the two incidents underscore the fragility of the npm ecosystem's trust model, where a single compromised maintainer token can cascade into millions of infected installations.
